moment_text: So I was always drawn to women's health. My mom actually works in the fertility space and I kind of was always like a little duckling right on her heels. It was, you know, I remember being in the fertility labs, you know, as a young kid throwing the scrubs on. They were way too big for me. And, uh, so I always wanted to be in women's health. And then, you know, I, I went to school to be a women's health nurse practitioner. I really liked the relationships that you were able to form with women over this man of their life. And then when it comes to menopause, you know, honestly I really just fell into that. I, I say I was sort of destined to be a women's health just from, you know, nepotism. But, uh, I sort of fell into the menopause space and it came on the heels of me going through a very difficult hormonal time in my postpartum journey of coming off a very challenging pregnancy. Having taken a, having taken a year off from work, moved to a new city. And so there was some identity crises going on in my life on top of a lot of the hormonal upheaval that was happening. And I really wanted to get back into the career space, um, and to see if I could kind of get, get some of that back. And, uh, an opportunity to came up to work with a gynecologist who had left obstetrical practice and had been working primarily in the women's midlife space for several years. And, you know, honestly I thought it was, is kind of silly. I, I, you know, it's just not something a lot of people, I think we hear a lot about it now. We really don't receive hardly any education in midlife care. I know we can beat that dead horse, but it just doesn't happen. And so the thought of working within a women's health clinic and only seeing women in mid-life was so foreign to me. Um, but I actually, I was really excited. It gave me a lot of flexibility. I only had to work, you know, one to two days a week, so I could still, my husband had a very demanding job where he traveled a lot, and so it gave me balance and I sort of just jumped in head first. I really liked the physician, uh, that was hiring for the job. And really the rest is history. I, I say once, don't you start taking care of midlife and menopausal patients. It's very addictive because you get a lot of wins and you don't always get a lot of wins in a lot of areas of medicine. Uh, and you get to see that immediate, that immediate gratification of. Help. Right. Um, so that's a, that's a long-winded answer, but it's kind of a, there's a storyline to it.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2 with barrier signals (education_gap, training_gap) related to a system interaction (medical_training), highlighting a systemic knowledge gap.

moment_text: So with natural menopause, your body has that long runway typically to adjust to the hormone changes over time. And that not only has immediate symptom impacts and immediate quality of life impacts, but it directly affects the trajectory of your long-term health as well. From a brain standpoint, a bone standpoint, a cardiovascular standpoint, I mean, those are the big heavy hitters.

moment_text: I think one of the big things, and we can go back to social media, is going around social media right now is a lot of negativity.
